Facts.Fact: One of the first major organized automobile events was the 1894 Paris-Rouen trial; the 1895 Paris-Bordeaux-Paris race is often cited as the first true long-distance speed race, at about 1,178 km (732 miles).
Formula 1 cars can typically accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in roughly 2.3 to 2.8 seconds, depending on setup and conditions.
The Indianapolis 500 began in 1911 at Indianapolis Motor Speedway and is one of the oldest major auto races in the world, though it was not held in some wartime years.
Ayrton Senna, a Brazilian racing driver, is widely considered one of the greatest Formula 1 drivers of all time; he won three World Championships before his death in 1994.
Modern Formula 1 cars use carbon-fiber composites extensively because they are very strong and lightweight.
Formula 1 power units are limited to 15,000 RPM by regulation, while many road-car engines redline around 6,000 to 7,000 RPM.
Aerodynamics are crucial in auto racing: car designs balance drag reduction with downforce to improve speed and handling.
Juan Manuel Fangio won five Formula 1 World Championships in the 1950s; his title record stood until Michael Schumacher won his sixth in 2003.
The 24 Hours of Le Mans is the world's oldest active endurance race, first run in 1923.
The Goodwood Festival of Speed is an annual hillclimb event in England featuring modern and historic competition cars and motorcycles.
The HANS (Head and Neck Support) device was introduced into top-level racing in the early 2000s to reduce serious head and neck injuries in crashes.
The SAFER (Steel and Foam Energy Reduction) barrier was developed to reduce impact severity and is used at many oval and speedway circuits.
Fire-resistant suits are mandatory in motorsport series; they are commonly made with materials such as Nomex.
The halo, introduced in Formula 1 in 2018, is a titanium cockpit-protection structure that has significantly improved driver safety.
The UK motorsport sector supports tens of thousands of jobs and contributes billions of pounds to the economy.
Top-level racing tires are often inflated with dry nitrogen to improve pressure consistency as temperatures change.
Sebastien Loeb, nine-time World Rally Champion, trained as a gymnast before becoming a rally driver.
Typical road tires may last roughly 35,000 to 60,000 miles, while racing tire life is much shorter and can range from tens to a few hundred miles depending on type and use.
Rear-view mirrors gained prominence in motorsport in the early 1900s and were later adopted broadly in road cars.
The first Formula 1 World Championship race was held at Silverstone in 1950.
Hybrid power has been central to modern Formula 1 since 2014, combining a turbocharged internal-combustion engine with energy-recovery electric systems.
Medical teams and extraction crews are standard at major race events, where rapid response is critical.
Formula 1 has a global broadcast footprint across roughly 180+ territories.
Touring car racing generally uses production-based cars that are modified for competition.
Formula 1 drivers can lose several kilograms of body mass in a race, especially in hot conditions; losses around 2 to 4 kg are possible.
A Formula 1 car can cost many millions of dollars to build, with estimates often in the low-to-mid tens of millions depending on accounting method.
Indianapolis Motor Speedway, opened in 1909, was the first purpose-built major speedway in the U.S. and was originally paved with about 3.2 million bricks.
Automobiles are among the most recycled consumer products, with most vehicle materials recoverable at end of life.
A typical modern vehicle contains on the order of 30,000 components when small parts are included.
At a constant 60 mph, driving the average Earth-Moon distance would take about 166 days (roughly five and a half months).
In the U.S., the broader auto industry supports around 10 million jobs when direct and indirect employment are combined.

Benz & Cie and Daimler Motoren Gesellschaft (maker of Mercedes cars) were competitors until 1926, when the two companies merged to form Daimler-Benz AG and began selling cars under the brand name 'Mercedes-Benz'.

The rubber used in the tires of a car is vulcanized. Vulcanization is a process for converting natural rubber or related polymers into more durable materials by heating them with sulfur or other equivalent curatives or accelerators.

Leonardo da Vinci sketched a design for a self-propelled cart around 1478 — one of the earliest known concepts for a wheeled vehicle driven without human or animal power. In 2004, a working replica was built by researchers at the Institute and Museum of the History of Science in Florence, Italy.

Ferrari deliberately limits production to preserve exclusivity; the company produces roughly 14,000 cars per year (about 40 per working day). By contrast, Toyota produces around 13,000 or more vehicles per day.
The year 1886 is considered to be the birth of the modern car. In that year, German inventor Carl Benz built a modern automobile called the Benz Patent-Motorwagen.
Inventor Mary Anderson (1866-1953) invented the first effective windshield wiper. They were initially considered a distraction.
In Christopher Nolan's film Batman Begins (2005), Bruce Wayne drives a Lamborghini Murciélago. In Spanish, murciélago means “bat.”

The BMW logo's blue and white quartered circle is derived from the colors of the Bavarian state flag. The popular idea that the design depicts a spinning propeller is a marketing story BMW promoted in later advertising.

Most Lamborghini car names are drawn from the world of bullfighting or famous fighting bulls. For example, Murciélago and Islero were the names of legendary bulls, and the Estoque is the sword used by matadors. A few names, like Countach (a Piedmontese exclamation), are exceptions to this pattern.
The word “car” is from the Latin carrum, which originally meant a “two-wheeled Celtic war chariot.” The Latin is further derived from the Proto-Indo-European root *kers- “to run.”
The first cars didn’t have a steering wheel. People had to steer them with a lever.
The best selling car of all time is the Toyota Corolla, with over 50 million sold worldwide.
The BMW M5 uses a system called Active Sound Design, which pipes and enhances engine sounds through the car's speakers to give drivers a more visceral connection to the engine's performance.
Bentley, Lamborghini, Audi, Ducati, and Porsche are all part of Volkswagen Group.
Over 250 million car tires are discarded each year.
Electric cars are not new. In fact, in 1900 38% of cars were electric, 40% were steam, and 22% were powered by gasoline.
The car company Chevrolet is named after the company’s co-founder Louis Chevrolet, a Swiss-born car racer.
The name “BMW” stands for Bayerische Motoren Werke, or Bavarian Motor Works.
The name “Hyundai” means “the present age” or “modernity” in Korean.
The car company Mazda’s name derives from “Ahura Mazda,” the Zoroastrian God of reason and intelligence. The stylized “M” in their logo symbolizes flight toward the future.
Mercedes’ three-point star in its logo represents its mission to make transportation easier on land, on water, and in the air.
Volvo is Latin for “I roll.” Its logo is a circle with an arrow pointing out, which is an ancient chemical symbol for iron. It is also one of the oldest ideograms in Western culture.
The intersecting ovals in the Toyota logo symbolize the trust between Toyota and its customers. The ovals also form the letter “T” for Toyota.
The name “Audi” is the Latin translation of the surname of the company’s founder, August Horch. 'Horch' means 'listen' in German, and 'Audi' means 'listen' in Latin.
The name “Nissan” is a contraction of 'Nippon Sangyo,' which means 'Japan Industries' in Japanese.

The name “Porsche” comes from Ferdinand Porsche, the legendary automotive engineer and designer. He founded Porsche AG in 1931 initially as an engineering and design consultancy (Porsche-Betrieb) before the company began manufacturing its own cars.

The name “Renault” comes Louis Renault, one of the company's co-founders. Renault brothers (Louis, Marcel, and Fernand) established the company in 1899 in France. Louis Renault was the primary driving force and visionary behind early innovations.
The name “Peugeot” comes from the Peugeot family. Armand Peugeot founded the automobile division in 1891, building on the Peugeot family's long manufacturing heritage in textiles and machinery.
